search.title: Reflective Refinement
search.summary: Start with one bounded draft, then tune the number and style of critique-and-revise loops instead of treating reflection as an unbounded “try again” instruction.
search.text: Reflective refinement is the depth-scaling pattern most teams reach for early. The tempting version is informal: ask the model to think again. The operational version is stricter. Reflection is a budgeted loop with explicit tuned variables governing how critique happens, what is remembered, and when the loop stops. The Practical Question The real question is not whether reflection sounds smart. It is whether one or two additional passes produce enough measurable lift to justify the extra cost and latency. That means tuning: - how many revision rounds are allowed - whether critiques come from a rubric, failure trace, or alternate perspective - which state is forwarded into the next round - which condition terminates the loop Reflection is not a personality trait of the agent. It is a governed controller with a finite budget and an explicit stop rule. Why It Works First-pass drafts often fail for systematic reasons: - they overlook a constraint - they optimize the wrong subgoal - they overfit to one interpretation of the prompt Reflection can help because it creates a structured chance to surface those mistakes before the system commits to output. Where Teams Go Wrong Reflection becomes wasteful when it is under-specified. Without a clear critique mode and stop criterion, the system often turns one useful pass into several near-duplicate passes. If the critique cannot point to a concrete defect or measurable rubric miss, extra rounds usually add style variation rather than substantive correction. Operational Checklist - Compare zero-round, one-round, and two-round settings explicitly. - Measure marginal lift per round rather than only best observed performance. - Keep critique prompts inspectable so failures can be diagnosed. - Treat “no further gain” as a valid stopping outcome, not as a failure to reflect. It has an explicit loop budget, a defined critique mechanism, and a stop rule that can be tested against cost, latency, and quality outcomes.

search.title: Judge Committees / Debate
search.summary: Introduce controlled disagreement among solvers or judges, then tune the committee size, rounds, and aggregation rule that convert disagreement into a release decision.
search.text: Judge committees and debate patterns introduce disagreement on purpose. Instead of trusting a single answer or a single evaluation pass, the system creates multiple views, then tunes how those views are compared, argued over, and collapsed into a decision. Two Common Variants 1. Solver-side debate: several solvers produce or defend answers over one or more rounds. 2. Judge-side committees: several evaluators score or rank candidate outputs, sometimes with dynamic team selection. The shared engineering idea is controlled disagreement plus governed aggregation. When correctness is hard to define procedurally, committees become a practical alternative to both single-sample outputs and constant human review. What Needs Tuning The pattern becomes expensive quickly, so its control surface matters: - how many agents or judges participate - how many rounds are allowed - whether agents see each other’s arguments directly - which rubric governs scoring - how ties, splits, or abstentions are handled What Makes It Operationally Tricky Disagreement is only useful if the system can tell whether it reflects: - real uncertainty about the task - genuine perspective diversity - or just noisy restatements of the same mistake That is why committee composition and aggregation rules should be visible in the evidence bundle, not buried in prompt text. A committee of nearly identical judges can create the appearance of rigor without improving calibration. Diversity source and aggregation policy matter at least as much as committee size. Operational Checklist - Measure agreement rate and disagreement resolution rate, not just final vote. - Log minority positions when promotion decisions are close. - Compare static committees with dynamic judge selection explicitly. - Decide ahead of time whether split decisions should promote, retry, or escalate. Because disagreement alone does not improve a system. The operational value comes from how that disagreement is interpreted, scored, and turned into a governed release or answer decision.
